EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, slow-moving low pressure system from the west provided just enough instability and general convection to allow this one brief weak landspout to form in an open field in Sheridan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous short wave trough and associated surface cold front moved rapidly across North Central Montana during early morning on the 6th. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was a concern with cold valley temperatures and a warm southwesterly flow moving over the region. A Winter Weather Advisory was issued at 2:16 PM with mention of freezing rain. The advisory was upgraded with more detail upon notification of road conditions becoming severe. Schools in Superior and Alberton were on 2 hour delays in the morning due to black ice on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT reported multiple slide-offs and jackknifed semis. Interstate 90 was categorized as Severe Driving Conditions by MDT at 4:57 AM between Tarkio and Alberton. Much of the rest of the zone had roads reported as either ice/black ice or snow covered. Due to the slick roadways, nearby schools were delayed for 2 hours in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: What began as light snow occurring along with a mix of freezing rain during the early morning of Jan 14th, 2016, quickly became prolonged wet, heavy snowfall in populated areas of northwest Montana. Several spotter calls were made, which revealed that power outages could become a real threat given the intensity and heaviness of new snowfall. Radar imagery and forecast guidance indicated intense snow would continue for a longer time period than originally expected, thus prompting the upgrade from a Winter Weather Advisory to a Winter Storm Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters in Bigfork, Montana reported 3 inches of very heavy, wet snow. Tree branches were being weighted down significantly, and people were unable to plow with an ATV due to the snow being very heavy. Power outages were reported around the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: What began as light snow occurring along with a mix of freezing rain during the early morning of Jan 14th, 2016, quickly became prolonged wet, heavy snowfall in populated areas of northwest Montana. Several spotter calls were made, which revealed that power outages could become a real threat given the intensity and heaviness of new snowfall. Radar imagery and forecast guidance indicated intense snow would continue for a longer time period than originally expected, thus prompting the upgrade from a Winter Weather Advisory to a Winter Storm Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Noisy Basin snotel recorded 2 feet of snow in 24 hours with 19 inches of it coming down in 12 hours. 3.2 inches of snow water equivalent was recorded in the Swan range which prompted the Flathead Avalanche Center issuing an Avalanche Warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a cold front across North Central Montana resulted in a very tight surface gradient. Strong northwest winds with gusts exceeding 60 mph were reported across portions of eastern North Central Montana during the early morning hours through the afternoon of April 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 63 mph was reported at Fort Belknap 13S (the Fort Belknap RAWS site).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system from the west stalled over the central and northern Rockies and pulled down colder air to change the precipitation over to all snow for the higher elevations of southwest Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Buckhorn General Store in Zortman, 2 to 6 inches of wet snow fell through most of the day.  Much of the snow compacted and melted on contact.  US Highway 191 through the area remained wet and not snow-covered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a short-wave trough from the southern Canadian Rockies, very strong winds in the mid-levels of the atmosphere were able to drop down to the surface with convective rain showers across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the McDonalds MT-13 DOT site, steady sustained winds of at least 40 mph were most prevalent for a little more than 1 hour during the middle of the day, but the verifying peak wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at 12:53 PM MST.  A peak wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Poplar mesonet site at 2:30 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rainfall and thunderstorms impacted Miles City on April 24, 2016. A rainfall total at the airport was recorded at 2.91 inches. This was a record daily rainfall. The old record was 2.71 inches dated June 18, 1964.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record daily rainfall event was recorded at the Miles City Airport. Rains totaled 2.91 inches for this calendar day. The previous record daily rainfall was 2.71 inches dated back on June 18, 1964.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation from a northern Rockies weather system moved across northeast Montana just ahead of a warm front.  The lingering surface temperatures below freezing set up perfect conditions for accumulating freezing rain and ice during the Saturday morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few slide-off vehicle accidents reported along with a light accumulation of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong disturbance, pushing a dry cold front through southern Saskatchewan suddenly dropped high winds from aloft all the way down to the surface over the northeast corner of Montana during the overnight and early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Navajo MT-5 DOT site measured sustained winds of 40 mph beginning at 12:38 AM. The verifying wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at 2:30 AM.  Sustained winds diminished below 40 mph after 2:40 AM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site measured sustained winds greater than 40 mph beginning at 2:09 AM and ending at 4:20 AM.  The first verifying wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at 2:50 AM and the peak wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at 3:00 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two people died in a fiery wreck involving a pickup and a semi on February 1st. The crash occurred on Interstate 90 near mile marker 430 just east of Park City, Montana about 945 pm. The occupants of the pickup died after the vehicle was passing slower traffic in the westbound lanes and the driver lost control in slick conditions. The pickup traveled through the median and into the eastbound lanes where it was struck by a semi. The pickup burst into flames. The semi driver, a 59-year-old Missoula man attempted to fight the flames with a fire extinguisher but could not save the people in the pickup.

A persistent snow band during the morning hours of February 2 led to severe driving conditions being declared for portions of I-90 in Mineral County, Montana. Favorable west to northwest uni-directional winds aloft lead to multiple quasi-stationary bands that created multiple-inch accumulations on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours, snow started falling over Lookout Pass and portions of I-90 in Mineral county. Around 10am, conditions reached a point where the MT Department of Transportation declared severe driving conditions for the section of I-90 from Lookout Pass to St. Regis, MT. These conditions lasted until the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep digging shortwave trough causes heavy and intense snowbands over the southern half of Flathead Lake. Road conditions quickly became snow covered, and severe driving conditions were reported on highway 93 and highway 35. Roads became impassible for trucks on highway 93, and roads were shutdown briefly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reported heavy, intense snow with little to no visibility over highway 93. Widespread 3 to 4 inches of snow with pockets of 4 to 6 inches of snow were reported along highway 93 near Big Arm. Multiple semi trucks were stuck on the side of the road and the highway was briefly shutdown due to severe driving conditions.

Roads were plowed and kept open with no accidents reported. Glacier National Park reported icy roads in the St Mary area but no incidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Circulation around a deep upper low moving across central Montana during the early morning hours of Friday, 15 April 2016, brought moisture and cooler air to the area. Snow became widespread during the morning across portions of North Central Montana during the morning with moderate to heavy rain in the warmer air across the Hi-Line. Gusty upslope winds and snowfall in excess of six inches caused areas of near blizzard conditions while rainfall exceeding two inches was also reported across portions of the Hi-Line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow resulted in over 40 power poles being downed across the area. Power was reported out for several hours at Chester.

Gusty upslope winds and snowfall in excess of six inches caused areas of near blizzard conditions while rainfall exceeding two inches was also reported across portions of the Hi-Line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow fell at Rocky Boy 9SE (the Rocky Boy SNOTEL site - elevation 4700 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Circulation around a deep upper low moving across central Montana during the early morning hours of Friday, 15 April 2016, brought moisture and cooler air to the area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell at Stanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and compact low pressure system from the northern Rockies spun through eastern Montana, bringing periods of moderate to heavy snow showers and high winds to many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 1W Flatwillow, pubic reported steel roof damage, tree limbs downed, transformers blown, and straw bales blown around with an estimated wind speed of 70 mph.  At the Winnett handar observation site, peak wind gusts of 61 mph were recorded multiple times through the late afternoon and early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a short-wave trough from the southern Canadian Rockies, very strong winds in the mid-levels of the atmosphere were able to drop down to the surface with convective rain showers across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A business owner reported roof damage to their building in Bainville.  Ten metal roofing sheets were dislodged and one sheet was blown off.  Estimated 60 mph wind gusts.  Event times and value of property damages are also estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a cold front across North Central Montana resulted in a very tight surface gradient. Strong northwest winds with gusts exceeding 60 mph were reported across portions of eastern North Central Montana during the early morning hours through the afternoon of April 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph were reported at Geraldine 8E for more than an hour. A gust to 56 mph was reported during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Circulation around a deep upper low moving across central Montana during the early morning hours of Friday, 15 April 2016, brought moisture and cooler air to the area. Snow became widespread during the morning across portions of North Central Montana during the morning with moderate to heavy rain in the warmer air across the Hi-Line. Gusty upslope winds and snowfall in excess of six inches caused areas of near blizzard conditions while rainfall exceeding two inches was also reported across portions of the Hi-Line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow was reported over portions of the Northern Rocky Mountain Front. The heavy snow and extremely poor driving conditions resulted in the cancellation of school at Heart Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Circulation around a deep upper low moving across central Montana during the early morning hours of Friday, 15 April 2016, brought moisture and cooler air to the area. Snow became widespread during the morning across portions of North Central Montana during the morning with moderate to heavy rain in the warmer air across the Hi-Line. Gusty upslope winds and snowfall in excess of six inches caused areas of near blizzard conditions while rainfall exceeding two inches was also reported across portions of the Hi-Line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow caused the downing of over 40 power poles across the area. Power was reported out for several hours at Valier and Conrad. Numerous slide-off vehicle accidents were also reported, especially on I-15. Many tree limbs were also reported broken in Conrad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Circulation around a deep upper low moving across central Montana during the early morning hours of Friday, 15 April 2016, brought moisture and cooler air to the area. Snow became widespread during the morning across portions of North Central Montana during the morning with moderate to heavy rain in the warmer air across the Hi-Line. Gusty upslope winds and snowfall in excess of six inches caused areas of near blizzard conditions while rainfall exceeding two inches was also reported across portions of the Hi-Line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nineteen inches of snow fell at Bynum 24W (the Mt Lockhart SNOTEL site - elevation 6400 feet). Also, sixteen inches of snow fell at Bynum 23W (the Waldron SNOTEL site - elevation 5600 feet). The heavy snow downed at least 40 power poles across the area causing power outages at Choteau, Fairfield, Dutton, and Power. School was canceled at Fairfield, Power, and Choteau. Numerous slide-off vehicle accidents were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific storm system and accompanied cold front brought high winds across portions of South Central Montana during the morning and afternoon hours of the 5th.  Although very little damage was reported, a few trees were downed. In addition, power lines were downed and a semi-truck was blown over on Interstate 90 near Big Timber.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 to 66 mph were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Circulation around a deep upper low moving across central Montana during the early morning hours of Friday, 15 April 2016, brought moisture and cooler air to the area. Snow became widespread during the morning across portions of North Central Montana during the morning with moderate to heavy rain in the warmer air across the Hi-Line. Gusty upslope winds and snowfall in excess of six inches caused areas of near blizzard conditions while rainfall exceeding two inches was also reported across portions of the Hi-Line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifteen inches of snow fell at Lincoln 11SE (the Nevada Ridge SNOTEL site - elevation|7020 feet). Also, twelve inches fell at Augusta 20N (the Wood Creek SNOTEL site - elevation 5960 feet). The heavy snow downed over 40 power poles across the area resulting in power outages in Augusta, Wolf Creek, and Craig. The Montana Highway Patrol responded to several slide-off and jackknife calls on I-15 in the Sieben Flats area. Numerous slide-offs also occurred on U.S. 287 between Helena and Three Forks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Circulation around a deep upper low moving across central Montana during the early morning hours of Friday, 15 April 2016, brought moisture and cooler air to the area. Snow became widespread during the morning across portions of North Central Montana during the morning with moderate to heavy rain in the warmer air across the Hi-Line. Gusty upslope winds and snowfall in excess of six inches caused areas of near blizzard conditions while rainfall exceeding two inches was also reported across portions of the Hi-Line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell at Monarch 5SSE. Also, seven inches of snow fell at Great Falls 5SW (the Great Falls ASOS site). Additionally, because of the heavy snow, 40 power poles were downed across the area resulting in power outages in Vaughn, Cascade, Ulm, Sun Prairie, Black Eagle, and Great Falls. Over a dozen slide-off vehicle crashes were reported throughout Cascade County. Two semi-tractor trailers jack-knifed on I-15 near Vaughn, blocking the northbound lanes. Many large tree limbs were also downed due to the heavy snow across Great Falls. Winds gusting to over 40 mph and visibilities less than one mile, caused near white-out conditions at times across Cascade County. The Great Falls Police Department issued a traffic alert via social media Twitter during the morning advising citizens to exercise extreme caution while driving.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific storm system and accompanied cold front brought high winds across portions of South Central Montana during the morning and afternoon hours of the 5th.  Although very little damage was reported, a few trees were downed. In addition, power lines were downed and a semi-truck was blown over on Interstate 90 near Big Timber.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at the Upper Musselshell Agrimet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and compact low pressure system from the northern Rockies spun through eastern Montana, bringing periods of moderate to heavy snow showers and high winds to many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Lufborough Hill DOT site, sustained winds of at least 40 mph lasted for only one hour during the middle of the day, but a verifying wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at 12:40 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Circulation around a deep upper low over South Dakota brought cooler air and abundant moisture to Blaine County during the morning and afternoon hours of 25 April. Snow, heavy at times, developed over the area with 8 to 10 inches reported some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow fell at Fort Belknap. Heavy wet snow fell across much of Blaine County. NorthWestern Energy reported 50 homes were without power due to downed power lines. Additionally, a semi-tractor trailer jackknifed on U.S. Highway 2, blocking both lanes of traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced severe hail just northeast of Billings.

An isolated thunderstorm produced severe hail just west of Crow Agency.

Mid level circulation over the Montana/Idaho border, as well as mid level circulation over southeast Montana, was responsible for a large plume of moisture that combined with cold air advecting into central Montana to produce areas of heavy snow on May 10. Snowfall amounts greater than 10 inches were reported in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighteen inches of snow fell at Rocky Boy 7SE and ten inches fell at Havre 25S.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level circulation over the Montana/Idaho border, as well as mid level circulation over southeast Montana, was responsible for a large plume of moisture that combined with cold air advecting into central Montana to produce areas of heavy snow on May 10. Snowfall amounts greater than 10 inches were reported in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at Stanford 3S. Heavy wet snow was reported on numerous roadways and scattered power outages were reported across Judith Basin county. The heavy wet snow required chains be used on towing units on Montana Highway 3 at Garneill to the junction of U.S.-87.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level circulation over the Montana/Idaho border, as well as mid level circulation over southeast Montana, was responsible for a large plume of moisture that combined with cold air advecting into central Montana to produce areas of heavy snow on May 10. Snowfall amounts greater than 10 inches were reported in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow fell at Neihart 6WSW, ten inches fell at Checkerboard 12ENE and  White Sulphur Springs 20SE, nine inches fell at White Sulphur Springs 18W, and eight inches fell at Neihart 12SSE. Severe driving conditions were reported throughout Meagher County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level circulation over the Montana/Idaho border, as well as mid level circulation over southeast Montana, was responsible for a large plume of moisture that combined with cold air advecting into central Montana to produce areas of heavy snow on May 10. Snowfall amounts greater than 10 inches were reported in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-two inches of snow fell at Gallatin Gateway 13ESE, sixteen inches fell at Gallatin Gateway 18SE, and eight inches fell at Belgrade 14NE. The heavy snow knocked out the power to five substations in Bozeman. NorthWestern Energy estimated some 30,000 customers, including Montana State University, lost power for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level circulation over the Montana/Idaho border, as well as mid level circulation over southeast Montana, was responsible for a large plume of moisture that combined with cold air advecting into central Montana to produce areas of heavy snow on May 10. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions reported on Montana Highway 66 near the junction with U.S. Highway 191. Power was also reported out in Hays and Lodgepole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level circulation over the Montana/Idaho border, as well as mid level circulation over southeast Montana, was responsible for a large plume of moisture that combined with cold air advecting into central Montana to produce areas of heavy snow on May 10. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-three inches of snow fell at Lewistown 11ESE. Also, seventeen inches fell at Garneill 12ENE and ten inches fell at Lewistown 2SE. Two semi-tractor trailers spun out on U.S.-87, 10 miles east of Lewistown, blocking the west bound lane. A semi-tractor trailer jackknifed on U.S.-87 15 miles west of Lewistown closing the road for an extended period of time. Severe driving conditions were also reported from Garneill to Judith Gap and from Lewistown to Hilger due to snow, wind, slush, and poor visibility. Severe driving conditions were reported near Eddie's Corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level circulation over the Montana/Idaho border, as well as mid level circulation over southeast Montana, was responsible for a large plume of moisture that combined with cold air advecting into central Montana to produce areas of heavy snow on May 10. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow fell at Neihart 1SE. The heavy wet snow caused a school bus to|slide off the road near Belt. No injuries were reported. Poor weather conditions in Great Falls caused the cancellation of the track meet scheduled between Great Falls High and C.M. Russell.

A strong upper level impulse moved over the Northern Rockies during the afternoon and evening hours of the 9th. Thunderstorms developed over the high terrain of western Montana and evolved into supercells and bowing segments as they moved across central and eastern Montana. These thunderstorms produced large hail and very strong wind gusts as they moved across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.

A moist southeast surface flow resulting in 50 to 60 degree dewoints along with strong deep shear combined with a strong mid-level speed max were centered over eastern Montana. As a result, severe thunderstorms developed across Rosebud, Custer and Fallon Counties. These thunderstorms produced very large hail, damaging wind gusts, as well as a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds flipped over a snowmobile trailer.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported golf ball size hail along with 50 mph winds.

Thunderstorms developed over the high terrain of western Montana and evolved into supercells and bowing segments as they moved across central and eastern Montana. These thunderstorms produced large hail and very strong wind gusts as they moved across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some siding was blown off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southeast surface flow resulting in 50 to 60 degree dewoints along with strong deep shear combined with a strong mid-level speed max were centered over eastern Montana. As a result, severe thunderstorms developed across Rosebud, Custer and Fallon Counties. These thunderstorms produced very large hail, damaging wind gusts, as well as a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The pre-storm environment for eastern Montana was such that severe weather was likely. Surface dew point values in the Baker area were in the lower 60s with strong southeasterly flow converging in the vicinity of a stationary surface trough. This area of strong surface convergence, which also separated moist rich air to the east and drier air to the west, played a critical role in the development of this short-lived tornado. ||Although no fatalities occurred , seven injuries, some requiring hospitalization, resulted from the tornado. Two homes were totally destroyed and 40 to 50 more sustained excessive damage. The city blocks located between South 5th Street East and South 10th Street East intersected by Texas Avenue were hit the hardest and sustained the most damage. Many power poles were broken resulting in power lines down and power outages. Single tree trunks were described to look like match sticks as they were stripped of all branches and leaves. ||Over 70 personnel came with boots on the ground from the surrounding tri-state area. The communities of Plevna, Ekalaka, and Wibaux, Montana, Marmarth, Rhame, Bowman and Beach, North Dakota, as well as Ludlow, South Dakota responded shortly after the tornado.  ||At the mesoscale level (2 to 2000 km in space), the interaction of pre-existing convergence zones or boundaries can serve as areas of storm development or enhancement. In some cases, pre-existing thunderstorms interacting with a boundary of converging surface winds can rapidly develop short-lived tornadoes with very little precursor indication from weather radar. The time scale of these events typically is on the order of a few minutes (0-10 minutes). ||In the case of the thunderstorms near Baker on Saturday, June 11th, evidence of these types of boundaries was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The North Tullock Wildfire was first reported Sunday, July 31st. Big Horn County firefighters were the first to respond before more resources arrived the next day from the Department of Natural Resources and Conservation (DNRC) Southern Land Office. The DNRC County Assist Team, Incident Commander assumed management of the wildfire on Tuesday, August 2nd. The fire was caused by lightning and was located 15 miles northeast of Hardin, Montana. The fire burned 2,720 acres of grass and timber. The wildfire was fully contained by 9 am, August 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Tullock Wildfire was first reported Sunday, July 31st. Big Horn County firefighters were the first to respond before more resources arrived the next day from the Department of Natural Resources and Conservation (DNRC) Southern Land Office. The DNRC County Assist Team, Incident Commander assumed management of the wildfire on Tuesday, August 2nd. The fire was caused by lightning and was located 15 miles northeast of Hardin, Montana. The fire burned 2,720 acres of grass and timber. The wildfire was fully contained by 9 am, August 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance within an unstable northwest flow aloft moved across eastern Montana during the afternoon and evening hours of the 27th. Aided by a strong jet streak, this disturbance resulted in a few long-lived super cell thunderstorms across the central and eastern portions of the Billings Forecast Area. These thunderstorms produced very large hail with the largest size being tennis ball size southeast of Colstrip, Montana in Rosebud County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong upper level disturbance combined with unstable conditions at the surface resulted in a round of severe thunderstorms across mainly Fallon County in the  Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon of the 6th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 2 inches in diameter along with 50 to 60 mph winds occurred at the Baker Airport. The hail was still two to three inches deep on the ground after the storm. Multiple hangers were damaged by the hail and wind. Several hangers received broken window damage. In addition, several vehicles at the airport were severely damaged with some windows of vehicles completely broken. A few of the vehicles were considered total losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southeast surface flow resulting in 50 to 60 degree dewoints along with strong deep shear combined with a strong mid-level speed max were centered over eastern Montana. As a result, severe thunderstorms developed across Rosebud, Custer and Fallon Counties. These thunderstorms produced very large hail, damaging wind gusts, as well as a tornado.

A cold front brought 40 to 50 mph wind gusts to the southern Bitterroot Valley, where falling branches led to short-lived power outages. Power companies were able to restore power to most customers within a couple hours, and to all customers by the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Deer Mountain RAWS reported a max wind gust of 50 mph between 12:06pm MST and 1:06 MST. Other locations above the valley floor gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range, including the Little Rock Creek RAWS with 45 mph gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance within an unstable northwest flow aloft moved across eastern Montana during the afternoon and evening hours of the 27th. Aided by a strong jet streak, this disturbance resulted in a few long-lived super cell thunderstorms across the central and eastern portions of the Billings Forecast Area. These thunderstorms produced very large hail with the largest size being tennis ball size southeast of Colstrip, Montana in Rosebud County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level impulse moved over the Northern Rockies during the afternoon and evening hours of the 9th. Thunderstorms developed over the high terrain of western Montana and evolved into supercells and bowing segments as they moved across central and eastern Montana. These thunderstorms produced large hail and very strong wind gusts as they moved across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.

A cold front brought 40 to 50 mph wind gusts to the southern Bitterroot Valley, where falling branches led to short-lived power outages. Power companies were able to restore power to most customers within a couple hours, and to all customers by the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility company reported via social media that a power outage affected a maximum of 2700 customers. Within 3 hours, all but 206 customers had power returned. These 206 customers regained power by the evening. Local media reported on the power outage shortly after the initial utility company report came out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Northern Rockies were under southwest flow ahead of a large low pressure system off the West Coast. A shortwave trough and associated jet streak were moving through the southwest flow providing upper level forcing for thunderstorms. Precipitable water values ranged from 0.75 to 1.00 inches, with deep shear of 50 kts, and CAPE values of 500 to 1000 J/kg. Short range models highlighted long-lived storm modes over the area. A convective watch was issued around 3 pm MDT highlighting an atmosphere favorable for strong thunderstorm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A business in Saltese, MT reported quarter or larger size hail. Winds blew down some tree limbs, and the hail dented cars. The worst part of the storm lasted about 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching strong low pressure system from the west combined with temperatures just hot enough to set of a few thunderstorms - one of which produced severe downdraft winds in southwest Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust was recorded at the Malta South US-191 DOT site.  Scattered thunderstorms were in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms impacted portions of the Billings Forecast Area during the early and mid afternoon hours of the 21st. Thunderstorms developed over the Beartooth/Absaroka Foothills and intensified as they moved off the foothills. One particular thunderstorm became severe over Central Carbon County before moving northeast into Yellowstone County. The storm impacted mainly the west and northwest sides of the city of Billings where hail ranged in size from pea to tennis ball size. There was a swath of golf ball size hail with a few the size of tennis balls across the northwest and north areas of the city of Billings.  Winds at the beginning of the storm gusted to 66 mph at the Billings Airport, but the majority of the hail core was not associated with these winds. Substantial damage to structures and vehicles was reported. In addition, three people were injured at the Billings Rod and Gun Club and treated for lacerations at the Billings Clinic. The storm caused two storm outages in Billings as firefighters responded to a few downed power and cable lines, mainly along and west and north of 24th Street West. In addition, some area car dealerships saw damage to their vehicles. ||The storm developed into a well-defined supercell just northeast of Billings in Yellowstone County and moved into northeast Yellowstone and southeast Musselshell Counties where a well-defined hook echo developed and a funnel cloud was reported. |This supercell thunderstorm then produced a tornado about 16 miles north of Pompeys Pillar located in northeast Yellowstone County. In addition, significant wind damage occurred south of the supercell track in clear air near the town of Hysham in Treasure County. This was likely the result of the cold pool propagating downstream along the Yellowstone River Valley.

A large upper-level low pressure system moving through the Great Basin and central Rockies regions, directed multiple periods of moderate rainfall, low clouds and weak instability over portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via social media, a cold air funnel amid low clouds and scattered rain showers in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough stretched from Saskatchewan to central California, allowing for ridging, clearing skies, and light winds over the Northern Rockies. All this occurred with an unseasonably cold air mass. This early cold spell with high 20s affected gardens and agriculture, as low temperatures reached values not typical until 2 or 3 weeks later in autumn. News media reported early harvest of crops near Missoula, including: lettuce, tomatoes, and squash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Libby, MT temperature sensor reported low of 29 F.

A deep trough stretched from Saskatchewan to central California, allowing for ridging, clearing skies, and light winds over the Northern Rockies. All this occurred with an unseasonably cold air mass. This early cold spell with high 20s affected gardens and agriculture, as low temperatures reached values not typical until 2 or 3 weeks later in autumn. News media reported early harvest of crops near Missoula, including: lettuce, tomatoes, and squash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters from the Potomac and Greenough, MT reported heavy frost with temperatures dropping to 20 F and possibly lower in the early morning hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado occurred about 16 miles north of Pompeys Pillar during the afternoon of the 21st. The tornado was on the ground for about 5 minutes and was 150 yards wide with a path length of 2 miles. Approximately 100 pine trees were either uprooted or snapped off. Two power poles were also snapped off with downed power lines. This tornado was rated an EF-1 with wind speeds of 90 to 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure trough approaching from the west set up a distinct dry-line boundary near the eastern edge of Montana and provided the focus for a few strong thunderstorms to develop - one of which became severe as it moved over Glendive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Off-duty NWS employee reported a 60 mph wind gust.

A closed low brought heavy snow to higher elevations of southwest Montana with short-term power outages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gem Mountain, located west of Georgetown Lake, reported 15 inches of heavy snow.

A backdoor cold front brought strong northerly winds to Flathead Lake during the afternoon and evening hours on May 12th negatively impacting a barge operation off of Finley Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flathead Lake Biological Station Narrows sensor reported an increase in northerly winds gusting to 41 mph at times by 8 pm MDT. Winds could have peaked closer to 50 mph over the open water towards the center of the lake. This northerly wind caused waves to grow over 26 miles of fetch reaching an estimated 6 to 8 feet at times. The waves knocked a shipping container off the stern of the barge and eventually the vessel sank. The bow of the barge was nosed onshore while the stabilizers were extended into the mud. With time, the waves hammered the legs down into the silt over time swamping the barge.

A slow moving closed low pressure system tracked from Oregon north and east across northwest Montana into southern Alberta from May 21st to May 22nd. This system most likely tapped into Gulf of Mexico moisture that had been pooling up over the High Plains and dropped 4 to 6 inches of rainfall to the mountains near Columbia Falls and Glacier National Park. This heavy rainfall caused area lakes and streams to overflow and flood nearby roads and land surrounding a few homes. This event also contributed to the West Glacier cooperative observer at Glacier National Park to report a record high precipitation total of 6.40 for the month of May, with records going back to 1949.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 to 6 inches of rain fell within 24 hours causing Spoon and Duck lakes to spill out onto Lake Drive and North Fork Road, and ending up near the Blankenship Road intersection as culverts were unable to handle the extra runoff. Rabe Road also  experienced flooding from water originating from the Tea Kettle Mountain area.  A private sensor located east-northeast of the Tea Kettle North Mountain or about 3 miles northwest of Coram, measured 6.29 inches of rainfall. The flooding closed North Fork Rd and Rabe Rd which impacted 25 to 30 residents. At least fifteen structures including basements and shop garages were flooded. One unfinished garage saw water that rose to near the ceiling. A fire chief with the Blankenship Fire Department who lives just off of North Fork Road outside of Columbia Falls reported that over three thousand gallons of water flooded his finished basement during this event. On May 23rd, more intense rain developed just north of Columbia Falls resulting in Trumble Creek coming out of its banks. This placed water around seven homes, but did not cause much damage or prevent residents from access. Glacier National Park closed the Going-to-the-Sun-Road at the Apgar Loop Junction due to a culvert washout in the afternoon of May 23rd.  Another reason why the culvert was overwhelmed was due to beaver dams breaking up higher in the drainage. At least two portions of the Forest Service road 895 past the Hungry Horse Dam were washed out on May 23rd including Beta Creek. Forest service road 316 from the 6.3 mile marker on North Fork Road to the Canyon Creek area also experienced several washouts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm moved rapidly northeast across far northwestern Treasure County. This storm then put out an outflow boundary that raced eastward across central Treasure County into the Yellowstone River Valley. An area of enhanced wind, estimated at 80 mph, developed in an area just north of Hysham northward to the Yellowstone River.  It���s estimated that upwards of 50 trees were either uprooted or snapped off. In addition, a mobile home was pushed off its blocks and a small garage (poorly constructed) was destroyed but contents left in place. Another home had numerous evergreens uprooted with damage to a garage and shed.

Warm, moist air from the southeast interacted with a low pressure system from the Pacific Northwest to generate multiple strong and severe thunderstorms which were focused mainly near the system's cold front as it passed through northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported quarter sized hail near Signal Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist air from the southeast interacted with a low pressure system from the Pacific Northwest to generate multiple strong and severe thunderstorms which were focused mainly near the system's cold front as it passed through northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 70 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to strong instability combined with strong wind shear resulted in a couple of supercell thunderstorms across far southeast Montana. One storm developed across Southern Big Horn County and became severe once it reached Powder River County. This thunderstorm lasted several hours as it moved across Powder River and Carter Counties before finally moving out of the state into South Dakota. This particular thunderstorm produced 4 diameter hail just northeast of Ridgeway.
A moist surface flow from the southeast combined with an unstable atmosphere near a stalled front to allow a few strong thunderstorms to develop, one of which became severe over Prairie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported ground covered in hail up to the size of hen eggs. Hail duration time was 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system from the southwest combined with a moist and unstable atmosphere to allow a few strong thunderstorms to develop - one of which became severe over Dawson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lindsay Divide MT-200 DOT station recorded a 62 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system moved eastward over the northern Rocky mountains and allowed stronger winds from above to mix down to the surface across portions of central and eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS station at Zortman measured a 60 mph wind gust sometime between 1:32 pm and 2:32 pm MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system from the Pacific moved slowly through the region while spreading several inches of precipitation across portions of northeast Montana, resulting in flooding across much of Phillips County which eventually moved down the Milk River system through Valley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Phillips County Emergency Manager reported many back country roads flooded, including Content Road, Sun Prairie Road, Beaverton Road. Regina Road south of Malta was underwater after a culvert on Alkali Creek washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system from the Pacific moved slowly through the region while spreading several inches of precipitation across portions of northeast Montana, resulting in flooding across much of Phillips County which eventually moved down the Milk River system through Valley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milk River near Malta (MALM8) rose above its flood stage of 16 feet at 3:27 AM MST on October 6th, crested at 18.10 feet at 01:38 AM MST on October 7th, then fell back down below flood stage at 12:10 AM MST on October 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system from the Pacific moved slowly through the region while spreading several inches of precipitation across portions of northeast Montana, resulting in flooding across much of Phillips County which eventually moved down the Milk River system through Valley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milk River near Saco (SACM8) rose above its flood stage of 20 feet at 8:14 PM MST on October 6th, crested at 21.61 feet at 3:27 PM MST on October 8th, then fell back down below flood stage at 4:55 PM MST on October 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system from the Pacific moved slowly through the region while spreading several inches of precipitation across portions of northeast Montana, resulting in flooding across much of Phillips County which eventually moved down the Milk River system through Valley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaver Creek near Hinsdale (BCHM8) rose above its minor flood stage of 14 feet at 12:24 AM MST on October 5th, then rose above its moderate flood stage of 16 feet at 5:48 AM MST on October 5th. It crested at 18.50 feet at 4:42 AM MST on October 6th, then fell back down below minor flood stage at 7:08 AM MST on October 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system from the Pacific moved slowly through the region while spreading several inches of precipitation across portions of northeast Montana, resulting in flooding across much of Phillips County which eventually moved down the Milk River system through Valley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milk River near Tampico (TMPM8) rose above its minor flood stage of 23 feet at 12:58 PM MST on October 6th, then rose above its moderate flood stage of 24 feet at 1:43 AM MST on October 7th. It crested at 25.75 feet at 3:24 AM MST on October 10th, then fell back down below minor flood stage at 9:42 PM MST on October 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that moved across the Baker area produced severe winds for about 10 minutes. However, no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 60 and 70 mph continuously for about 10 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm took out a calving shed along with several windows in two homes. Other homesteads across the area also suffered some broken windows and siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms tracked from Lemhi County, Idaho north to the Missoula Valley bringing periods of strong gusty winds. The air mass was hot and dry which contributed to the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula dispatch reported a lot of tree limbs down around town with numerous power lines down. A couple of lightning strikes started fires with one strike to a house in Missoula and a field in Florence. A private weather sensor located southeast of Lolo recorded a wind gust to 60 mph ahead of the main thunderstorm cells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed over the southern Bitterroot Valley as an upper level low approached the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail reported on the south side of the Roaring Lion Fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain across the city of Great Falls and portions of Cascade County during the early evening hours of August 9. Some parts of Great Falls received more than 2 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public and law enforcement reports numerous cars stranded due to high water with several people rescued from their cars. Several roads were closed due to high water and at least 4 businesses along with 25 residences reported damage due to water. People were evacuated from an apartment complex and several buildings on the campus of the University of Great Falls suffered water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong back-door cold front pushed south out of Canada and brought frequent wind gusts over 50 mph at times across portions of western Montana. It also triggered thunderstorms, one of which caused damage in the Missoula Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public located in Missoula tweeted a photo of hail that appeared to be quarter size. There were multiple reports of nickel sized hail with some damage to gardens and vegetation. Intense rainfall was also produced by this storm, and the greater impact may have been street ponding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the Billings forecast area on the 14th. Rapid pressure rises and tight pressure gradients behind the front resulted in high wind gusts across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at the Judith Gap DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough located along the west coast was responsible for a moist and unstable southwest flow into Montana. This southwest flow aloft was tapping into a large plume of monsoonal moisture which was present over the desert southwest. The combination of moisture, an advancing cold front, and an airmass that was rapidly becoming unstable, thunderstorms developed across portions of North Central Montana. Some storms become severe with large hail and heavy rain the primary concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped 1 inch diameter hail at Monarch 5SSE and at Neihart 7NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The North Tullock Wildfire was first reported Sunday, July 31st. Big Horn County firefighters were the first to respond before more resources arrived the next day from the Department of Natural Resources and Conservation (DNRC) Southern Land Office. The DNRC County Assist Team, Incident Commander assumed management of the wildfire on Tuesday, August 2nd. The fire was caused by lightning and was located 15 miles northeast of Hardin, Montana. The fire burned 2,720 acres of grass and timber. The wildfire was fully contained by 9 am, August 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday, October 9th, a cold front advanced southward across north-central MT. Behind this front, easterly to northeasterly surface winds underwent orographic lift along the Rocky Mountain Front. Farther aloft, moist southwesterly flow underwent isentropic lift ahead of an approaching Pacific shortwave trough. This complex flow pattern produced significant snow accumulations along the Rocky Mountain Front and portions of the Hi-Line on Monday, October 10th. As the aforementioned shortwave trough advanced eastward, significant snowfall also impacted Gallatin County in southwest Montana, including Bozeman, on Tuesday, October 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimates 4-inches of snowfall in Cut Bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river of moisture combined with a backdoor cold front lead to heavy snow for the mountains across Glacier Park and the Bob Marshall Wilderness Area.  The heavy wet snow and strong winds caused multiple trees down on Highway 2 east of West Glacier. Also, Flathead Electric reported over 32 outages due to the winter storm. This was the first big snowfall event of the season for the mountains and the first snowfall for many valleys in northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation reported 10 inches of snow at Marias Pass and multiple trees down on Highway 2 due to heavy wet snow and strong gusty winds. The Going to the Sun Road was also closed due to the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday, October 9th, a cold front advanced southward across north-central MT. Behind this front, easterly to northeasterly surface winds underwent orographic lift along the Rocky Mountain Front. Farther aloft, moist southwesterly flow underwent isentropic lift ahead of an approaching Pacific shortwave trough. This complex flow pattern produced significant snow accumulations along the Rocky Mountain Front and portions of the Hi-Line on Monday, October 10th. As the aforementioned shortwave trough advanced eastward, significant snowfall also impacted Gallatin County in southwest Montana, including Bozeman, on Tuesday, October 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer 4 SSE of Bynum reported 7.3 inches of snow in past 24-hours. It was still snowing at time of report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: KBZK-TV in Bozeman reported that the MT Highway Patrol reported a fatal crash on I-90 near mile marker 280, just east of Three Forks. Roadways were slick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid- to upper-level low moved northeastward from SW WY on the afternoon of Monday, October 3rd to southeast SK by early evening of Wednesday, October 5th. Simultaneously, the accompanying surface low moved from southeast MT to southern MB. Cold air advection and dynamic cooling on the backside of the storm system allowed rain to change to snow at some lower elevation locations east of Interstate 15 in north-central MT on the evening of Monday, October 3rd into the morning of Tuesday, October 4th. Elevations near and above 5000 feet MSL in the same part of north-central MT experienced snow throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crystal Lake SNOTEL (15 mi SE of Moore) at an elevation of 6050 feet reported 24-hour snowfall of 15-inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough embedded in strong westerly flow aloft, combined with a tight horizontal surface pressure gradient accompanying a lee surface trough east of the Alberta and Montana Rockies, created a favorable environment for strong and gusty winds along Montana's Rocky Mountain Front and over the plains immediately east of the mountains. In addition, strong southerly winds impacted the Madison Valley as airflow funneled through the valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Deep Creek RAWS reported peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system from the pacific northwest stalled over southern Montana and northern Wyoming with plentiful moisture. Significant amounts of moderate and heavy rain spread across many locations while enough cold air from the Canadian Rockies wrapped around the system to change the precipitation to a heavy, very wet snow for some higher elevations of central and northern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rainfall as well as 2 to 3 feet of snow that eventually melted contributed to flooding in the southern portions of Phillips County.  Beaver Creek remained in it's banks south of Saco, but as it got closer to Saco the water left it's banks, and at least 100 yards of the Beaverton Road was underwater. Peoples Creek near Dodson was flooded out of it's banks in the Dodson area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system from the pacific northwest stalled over southern Montana and northern Wyoming with plentiful moisture. Significant amounts of moderate and heavy rain spread across many locations while enough cold air from the Canadian Rockies wrapped around the system to change the precipitation to a heavy, very wet snow for some higher elevations of central and northern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employees driving along US Highway 200 through western Garfield County reported whiteout conditions with blowing and drifting snow during the evening hours on May 10th.  County emergency officials later reported 24 to 36 inches of snow and 60 power poles snapped.  Event times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the seasons first major winter storm and arctic outbreak. An arctic front combined with abundant Pacific moisture resulted in very heavy snowfall across South Central and Southeast Montana. Snow accumulations of 8+ inches occurred for most areas along and west of a Roundup-Billings-Sheridan line. Billings received 12-17 inches, Livingston 8-12 inches and Sheridan 6-8 inches of snow. Severe driving conditions were common across the south central areas. Numerous school closures occurred on the 16th. The storm also prompted the closure of Zoo Montana on the 15th and 16th. In addition, numerous accidents occurred on local interstates and highways. The more significant impacts with this storm occurred after the snow had ended as strong winds developed across portions of the area creating blizzard conditions. For more information, please see the event titled Blizzard December 17-19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches was reported across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Navajo MT-5 DOT site recorded wind chill values between -40 and -48 through the evening hours of the 16th and the morning hours of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved out of the Central Rockies into the central plains on Christmas Eve and into Christmas Day. This low resulted in tight pressure gradients across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas. In addition, the low wrapped abundant moisture out of the Gulf of Mexico north and westward across southeast Montana. As a result, heavy snow fell across Rosebud, Custer, Fallon, Powder River, Carter and portions of Southern Big Horn Counties, as well as Sheridan County in Wyoming. In addition, the tight pressure gradients resulted in very gusty northwest winds which created blizzard conditions across these areas. The following impacts resulted from this major winter storm: I-90 was closed from Sheridan, Wyoming to the South Dakota line on Christmas Day. Periodic road closures occurred across southeast Montana on Highways US212, US12, MT39 and MT59.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow drifts 2 to 5 feet, along with very gusty winds with 1/4 mile visibilities were reported across the area. Severe travel conditions were also reported. Highway 212 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front combined with abundant Pacific moisture resulted in very heavy snowfall over a 2-day period. Snow accumulations of 8+ inches occurred from Billings and areas westward. The biggest impacts with this storm occurred after the snow ended as lee-side troughing developed. This resulted in very tight pressure gradients across the Upper Yellowstone and Stillwater valleys. In addition, very strong winds aloft, around 50kts, mixed down to the surface across these areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved out of the Central Rockies into the central plains on Christmas Eve and into Christmas Day. This low resulted in tight pressure gradients across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas. In addition, the low wrapped abundant moisture out of the Gulf of Mexico north and westward across southeast Montana. As a result, heavy snow fell across Rosebud, Custer, Fallon, Powder River, Carter and portions of Southern Big Horn Counties, as well as Sheridan County in Wyoming. In addition, the tight pressure gradients resulted in very gusty northwest winds which created blizzard conditions across these areas. The following impacts resulted from this major winter storm: I-90 was closed from Sheridan, Wyoming to the South Dakota line on Christmas Day. Periodic road closures occurred across southeast Montana on Highways US212, US12, MT39 and MT59.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow drifts 2 to 5 feet, along with very gusty winds with 1/4 mile visibilities were reported across the area. Severe travel conditions were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the seasons first major winter storm and arctic outbreak. An arctic front combined with abundant Pacific moisture resulted in very heavy snowfall across South Central and Southeast Montana. Snow accumulations of 8+ inches occurred for most areas along and west of a Roundup-Billings-Sheridan line. Billings received 12-17 inches, Livingston 8-12 inches and Sheridan 6-8 inches of snow. Severe driving conditions were common across the south central areas. Numerous school closures occurred on the 16th. The storm also prompted the closure of Zoo Montana on the 15th and 16th. In addition, numerous accidents occurred on local interstates and highways. The more significant impacts with this storm occurred after the snow had ended as strong winds developed across portions of the area creating blizzard conditions. For more information, please see the event titled Blizzard December 17-19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall around 6 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system from the pacific northwest stalled over southern Montana and northern Wyoming with plentiful moisture. Significant amounts of moderate and heavy rain spread across many locations while enough cold air from the Canadian Rockies wrapped around the system to change the precipitation to a heavy, very wet snow for some higher elevations of central and northern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Rocky Mountains near Zortman received 24 inches of heavy wet snow and wind speeds of 35 to 50 mph for several hours on May 10th.  Numerous tree were down and dozens of power poles were snapped.  Snow drifts up to 14 feet high were reported.  Emergency evacuations were initiated at the Landusky Mine. Up tp 40 power poles were snapped due to the heavy, wet snow and the strong winds.  Two small communities were without power for at least 24 hours.

The more significant impacts with this storm occurred after the snow had ended as strong winds developed across portions of the area creating blizzard conditions. For more information, please see the event titled Blizzard December 17-19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area SNOTELS received around 12 of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system from the pacific northwest stalled over southern Montana and northern Wyoming with plentiful moisture. Significant amounts of moderate and heavy rain spread across many locations while enough cold air from the Canadian Rockies wrapped around the system to change the precipitation to a heavy, very wet snow for some higher elevations of central and northern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 2 to 4.5 inches of rainfall occurred across the county from May 9th through the 12th, with Glasgow to Fort Peck Dam area seeing the highest totals.  Flood waters inundated a ranch on Antelope Creek.  Water went across the Tampico Highway west of Glasgow, and several gravel roads were under water west of Glasgow and south of Hinsdale from smaller creeks in the area.  One pickup truck was swept downstream a ways along the Billingsley Rd and the Tampico Highway when it encountered deep waters at the intersection.  A stock dam also broke on the Maxness Road near Fort Peck.  It caused no property damage according to law enforcement.||Beaver Creek near Hinsdale crested at 16.74 feet around 1 am on Friday May 13th.  Flood stage is 14 feet.  It hit 14 feet around 5 am Thursday May 12th.  It went below flood stage around 1 am Saturday May 16th.  ||The Milk River at Glasgow hit flood state around noon on Thursday May 12th and peaked around 15.62 feet on Friday May 13th.  It dropped below flood stage 24 hours later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anywhere from 9 inches of snow by Marias Pass to 13.5 inches at West Glacier fell with this storm. An Arctic cold front plowed through Marias Pass which caused temperatures to drop from the middle 20's to single digits below zero in a matter of two hours. Combined with winds gusting near 30 mph, the wind chills ranged between -20 and -30 degrees. Blowing snow was reported by MDT during the night along US 2. The Arctic cold front lost steam as it made its way toward Columbia Falls and the Flathead Valley where only 2 to 3 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A skier was killed by an avalanche on Henderson Mountain outside Cooke City on December 11th. The skier was in a party of 5 and his descent was the 7th track on the slope when it avalanched. He was carried into trees and buried under 5' of snow. Another person was buried to his waist, uninjured, as he stood in the runout zone. The victim was found with avalanche transceivers and dug up in 15 to 20 minutes. CPR was initiated, but unsuccessful as trauma was a likely factor in his death. For a more detailed report, please see the avalanche incidents report at http://www.mtavalanche.com.

CPR was initiated, but unsuccessful as trauma was a likely factor in his death. For a more detailed report, please see the avalanche incidents report at http://www.mtavalanche.com.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted up to 79 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lee-side troughing continued across south central Montana on the 19th and 20th. After blizzard conditions the previous days, no significant impacts occurred as a result of these winds as the snow composition was such that significant blowing and drifting was not a factor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 60 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of recent heavy snow with high winds generated severe driving conditions at Marias Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow with amounts between 9 and 12 inches combined with wind gusts near 50 mph to generate blowing and drifting snow between Essex and Marias Pass. The Montana Department of Transportation stated that there were severe driving conditions in the aforementioned area along US-2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an unusually warm November, this event was a large change to cold and wintry conditions for travelers and hunters alike. A warm front followed by a cold front brought periods of heavy snow that caused slick driving conditions to western Montana. Cold temperatures developed at the end of the event contributing to solidifying moisture on the roads and slick roads for the morning commute on Dec. 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lost Trail Pass received 8 inches of snow while Lolo Pass saw 13 inches of snow during this event. A strong cold front moved through between the afternoon and evening of Dec. 4th which initiated gusty winds and temperatures dropping into the teens at Lolo Pass and single digits at Lost Trail. The combination of fresh snow, dropping temperatures and wind chills approaching 15 degrees below zero caused dangerous conditions in the back-country for hunters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust shortwave brought heavy mountain snow, mixed precipitation and strong gusty winds. Cold surface temperatures contributed to icy conditions in valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass received 13 inches of snow during the event, and there were reports of whiteout conditions with blowing and drifting snow. A worker at the pass reported that it was the worst conditions he had seen in 13 years. The winds were so strong that a tree had blown into the west bound lane north of the pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between one to two feet of snow fell in the mountains during this event. Gusty winds contributed to blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow combined with frequent wind gusts to 32 mph to generate blowing and drifting snow along US-2 between West Glacier and Marias Pass. By the morning of the 28th, the Montana Department of Transportation reported severe driving conditions leading up to Marias Pass. Total storm snowfall ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved quickly east-southeastward from west-central British Columbia on the morning of Tuesday, December 20th and redeveloped over southern Alberta, in the lee of the Canadian Rockies, by Tuesday afternoon before moving farther to the east. Westerly and moist flow accompanying this system allowed upslope snow, heavy at times, to spill-over the Continental Divide and impact portions of the northern Rocky Mountain Front, including Marias Pass along US-2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT reported severe winter driving conditions along US-2 from Browning to Marias Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved out of the Central Rockies into the central plains on Christmas Eve and into Christmas Day. This low resulted in tight pressure gradients across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas. In addition, the low wrapped abundant moisture out of the Gulf of Mexico north and westward across southeast Montana. As a result, heavy snow fell across Rosebud, Custer, Fallon, Powder River, Carter and portions of Southern Big Horn Counties, as well as Sheridan County in Wyoming. In addition, the tight pressure gradients resulted in very gusty northwest winds which created blizzard conditions across these areas. The following impacts resulted from this major winter storm: I-90 was closed from Sheridan, Wyoming to the South Dakota line on Christmas Day. Periodic road closures occurred across southeast Montana on Highways US212, US12, MT39 and MT59.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 9 inches was reported. Highway 212 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved out of the Central Rockies into the central plains on Christmas Eve and into Christmas Day. This low resulted in tight pressure gradients across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas. In addition, the low wrapped abundant moisture out of the Gulf of Mexico north and westward across southeast Montana. As a result, heavy snow fell across Rosebud, Custer, Fallon, Powder River, Carter and portions of Southern Big Horn Counties, as well as Sheridan County in Wyoming. In addition, the tight pressure gradients resulted in very gusty northwest winds which created blizzard conditions across these areas. The following impacts resulted from this major winter storm: I-90 was closed from Sheridan, Wyoming to the South Dakota line on Christmas Day. Periodic road closures occurred across southeast Montana on Highways US212, US12, MT39 and MT59.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was reported. MT Highway 39 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an unusually warm November, this event was a large change to cold and wintry conditions for travelers and hunters alike. A warm front followed by a cold front brought periods of heavy snow that caused slick driving conditions to western Montana. Cold temperatures developed at the end of the event contributing to solidifying moisture on the roads and slick roads for the morning commute on Dec. 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of light to heavy snow fell during this event. A few places did mix with rain in the afternoon of Dec. 4th. While over a foot of snow fell in the mountains, Lindbergh Lake saw 11.6 inches and Seeley Lake saw 3.7 inches of total snowfall. Once the cold front passed through later Sunday, temperatures fell into the teens by Monday morning causing slick spots on the roads. The combination of fresh snow and cold temperatures created dangerous conditions in the back-country.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several heavy snow bands, with snow rates up to 5 inch per hour, developed during the day of Dec. 4th then a weak Arctic cold front tracked south during the evening causing widespread snow. As this front lost its steam towards Flathead Lake a heavy snow band formed and impacted much of Highway 35 towards the northeastern Mission Valley. Above freezing temperatures during the day contributed to a snow-over-ice situation by the evening, and very slick conditions lasted into the next morning. Total snowfall amounts include: 9 Columbia Falls, 5.5 Bigfork, 4.6 Glacier Park International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system from the pacific northwest stalled over southern Montana and northern Wyoming with plentiful moisture. Significant amounts of moderate and heavy rain spread across many locations while enough cold air from the Canadian Rockies wrapped around the system to change the precipitation to a heavy, very wet snow for some higher elevations of central and northern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employees driving along US Highway 200 reported whiteout conditions with blowing and drifting snow during the evening hours on May 10th. A semi-truck slid off the road, temporarily closing it due to the icy conditions and strong winds.

As a result, heavy snow along with blowing and drifting snow, as well as significantly reduced visibilities were reported across portions of southeast Montana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the late afternoon into the evening hours on the 16th. This resulted in a cold, moist easterly upslope flow over the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills. At the same time, an upper low moved across southern Wyoming providing additional upslope flow to the mountain locations. As a result, significant snow accumulations occurred over the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 7 inches were reported across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Goldstone CWOP site measured a 60 mph wind gust associated with the cold front passage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet and progressive storm system from the Pacific Northwest combined with a push of colder air from the Canadian Rockies to produce accumulating and blowing snow across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-operative observers and trained spotters across most of Richland County reported 5 to 6 inches of new snowfall with the greatest amounts over the Big Sheep Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet and progressive storm system from the Pacific Northwest combined with a push of colder air from the Canadian Rockies to produce accumulating and blowing snow across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter in northwest Dawson County reported 7 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front swept southward across North-Central MT during the afternoon of Sunday, December 11th. Broad lift and sufficient moisture along and behind this front allowed widespread snow to impact much of North-Central MT, including the Rocky Mountain Front, Sunday into Monday, December 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches for storm-total snowfall at Pike Creek SNOTEL (11 miles SSW of East Glacier Park).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice jam flooding was reported along the Madison River, near Ennis, on the early afternoon of December 27th. The jam also caused flooding along Odell Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported an ice jam on the Madison River causing flooding near Ennis. The flooding was occurring along portions of the Madison River and Odell Creek. Water was covering the US-287 bridge near Ennis, at mile marker 48.2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Zonal flow aloft across the Northern Rockies strengthened in response to a positively-tilted shortwave trough diving southeastward over British Columbia. This, in turn, caused a lee surface trough to strengthen over North-Central MT during the early morning hours of Saturday, December 31st. The tightening pressure gradient primarily resulted in strong, sub-warning-criteria peak wind gusts. However, the Cut Bank Airport ASOS reported a peak gust of 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cut Bank ASOS reported a peak gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee-side low pressure trough and moist southeast surface winds combined with an unstable atmosphere to give rise to some organized and efficient thunderstorms - a few of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 60 mph wind gusts along with dime sized hail and heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the late afternoon into the evening hours on the 16th. This resulted in a cold, moist easterly upslope flow over the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills. At the same time, an upper low moved across southern Wyoming providing additional upslope flow to the mountain locations. As a result, significant snow accumulations occurred over the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area Snotels reported around 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong surface low moved northeast out of Colorado into the eastern Dakotas and western Minnesota. This low pulled moisture northward from the Gulf of Mexico and wrapped it around the low westward into the western Dakotas and the far eastern counties of Montana. In addition, tight pressure gradients across eastern Montana resulted in very gusty northwest winds. As a result, heavy snow along with blowing and drifting snow, as well as significantly reduced visibilities were reported across portions of southeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 6 inches, blowing and drifting snow and significantly reduced visibilities were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough stretched from Saskatchewan to central California, allowing for ridging, clearing skies, and light winds over the Northern Rockies. All this occurred with an unseasonably cold air mass. This early cold spell with high 20s affected gardens and agriculture, as low temperatures reached values not typical until 2 or 3 weeks later in autumn. News media reported early harvest of crops near Missoula, including: lettuce, tomatoes, and squash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kalispell International Airport saw a low of 27 degrees while a temperature sensor in Ronan recorded a low of 25 degrees Fahrenheit.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor in Seeley Lake reported a low of 21 degrees F. Spotters from the Potomac and Greenough areas reported heavy frost with temperatures dropping to 20 degrees F and possibly lower in the early morning hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures fell below freezing across northwest Montana including 25 deg. F at Eureka, 22 deg. F at Yaak and 29 deg. F in Libby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough stretched from Saskatchewan to central California, allowing for ridging, clearing skies, and light winds over the Northern Rockies. All this occurred with an unseasonably cold air mass. This early cold spell with high 20s affected gardens and agriculture, as low temperatures reached values not typical until 2 or 3 weeks later in autumn. News media reported early harvest of crops near Missoula, including: lettuce, tomatoes, and squash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local media in Missoula reported that local farms took steps to harvest tomatoes, lettuce, and squash early because of the forecast sub-freezing temperatures. Temperatures dropped to 30 deg. F at the Missoula International Airport and to 28 deg. F in Stevensville.
